abstract: Building generalist embodied agents requires integrating perception, language understanding, and action, which are core capabilities addressed by Vision-Language-Action (VLA) approaches based on multimodal foundation models, including recent advances in vision-language models and world models. Despite rapid progress, VLA methods remain fragmented across incompatible architectures, codebases, and evaluation protocols, hindering principled comparison and reproducibility. We present StarVLA, an open-source codebase for VLA research. StarVLA addresses these challenges in three aspects. First, it provides a modular backbone--action-head architecture that supports both VLM backbones (e.g., Qwen-VL) and world-model backbones (e.g., Cosmos) alongside representative action-decoding paradigms, all under a shared abstraction in which backbone and action head can each be swapped independently. Second, it provides reusable training strategies, including cross-embodiment learning and multimodal co-training, that apply consistently across supported paradigms. Third, it integrates major benchmarks, including LIBERO, SimplerEnv, RoboTwin~2.0, RoboCasa-GR1, and BEHAVIOR-1K, through a unified evaluation interface that supports both simulation and real-robot deployment. StarVLA also ships simple, fully reproducible single-benchmark training recipes that, despite minimal data engineering, already match or surpass prior methods on multiple benchmarks with both VLM and world-model backbones. To our best knowledge, StarVLA is one of the most comprehensive open-source VLA frameworks available, and we expect it to lower the barrier for reproducing existing methods and prototyping new ones. StarVLA is being actively maintained and expanded; we will update this report as the project evolves. The code and documentation are available at this https URL.

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "StarVLA: A Lego-like Codebase for Vision-Language-Action Model Developing."

---

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The primary authors of this paper are Jinhui Ye, Ning Gao, Yilun Chen, Weiyu Guo, Zixuan Wang, Yuxing Chen, Fangjing Wang, Senqiao Yang, Chengyao Wang, Yuqi Liu, Meng Chu, Changsheng Lu, Pengguang Chen, Shu Liu, and Jiaya Jia. The affiliations listed are the StarVLA Community and the Von Neumann Institute, HKUST. Yilun Chen, Shu Liu, and Jiaya Jia are designated as corresponding authors.

---

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The development of general-purpose embodied agents that can integrate perception, language understanding, and action in physical environments represents a significant area of research in artificial intelligence. Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, often built upon multimodal foundation models, have emerged as a dominant paradigm for this objective. These models typically encompass two main families: VLM-based methods, which leverage the representational capacities of language models for action decoding, and world-model-based methods, which utilize generative architectures to model action distributions and future observations.

Despite advances in VLA methods, the broader research landscape is characterized by fragmentation. This fragmentation manifests at multiple levels:
*   **Architectural Diversity**: Existing VLA approaches employ varied action-decoding designs, ranging from VLM-native techniques (e.g., autoregressive tokenization, parallel regression) to generative-model-based approaches (e.g., diffusion, flow matching). These diverse designs are often incompatible, complicating systematic comparison and trade-off analysis.
*   **System-Level Incompatibility**: Methods are typically released with tightly coupled assumptions regarding model architecture, data processing, and training pipelines. This limits component reuse and makes it challenging to integrate different parts across projects.
*   **Evaluation Inconsistency**: Results are frequently reported on disjoint subsets of benchmarks using inconsistent protocols, which hinders fair comparison and impedes reproducibility.

This collective fragmentation creates a barrier to systematic exploration, comparative analysis, and the cumulative development of embodied AI systems. Existing open-source codebases are largely method-specific and do not uniformly support modular composition, reusable training across heterogeneous data sources, or standardized evaluation and deployment. StarVLA positions itself within this landscape by addressing these fragmentation issues, aiming to provide a unified, open-source platform that brings together diverse VLA paradigms under a common, modular framework.

---

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The central objective of the StarVLA project is to facilitate the development of generalist embodied agents by providing a unified, modular, and open-source codebase for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) research. This objective is motivated by the recognized challenges posed by the fragmented nature of current VLA research.

The key motivations and detailed objectives are as follows:

1.  **Addressing Fragmentation and Incompatibility**: The primary motivation stems from the observation that VLA methods are disparate, employing incompatible architectures, codebases, and evaluation protocols. This impedes systematic comparison, slows down research progress, and complicates the reproduction of results. StarVLA aims to counteract this by offering a standardized platform.
2.  **Enabling Systematic Comparison of VLA Paradigms**: Current VLM-based and world-model-based VLA approaches are often developed in isolation. A core objective is to allow direct and principled comparison between these distinct paradigms. StarVLA achieves this by providing a unified abstraction where different VLM and world-model backbones, as well as various action-decoding mechanisms, can be swapped and evaluated under identical training and evaluation conditions.
3.  **Promoting Modularity and Component Reuse**: Existing VLA codebases often integrate model architecture, data processing, and training pipelines in a tightly coupled manner, limiting the reuse of individual components. StarVLA's objective is to introduce a modular architecture—specifically, a backbone–action-head decomposition—that allows independent swapping of the vision-language backbone and the action head. This design is intended to increase flexibility for prototyping and experimentation.
4.  **Standardizing Training Strategies**: Diverse training strategies, such as cross-embodiment learning and multimodal co-training, are often implemented as method-specific add-ons. StarVLA aims to integrate these as reusable, paradigm-agnostic configurations, making it easier for researchers to study their interactions with different architectural choices.
5.  **Unifying Evaluation and Deployment Protocols**: The lack of consistent evaluation protocols across different benchmarks makes fair comparisons difficult. StarVLA seeks to integrate major VLA benchmarks (e.g., LIBERO, SimplerEnv, RoboTwin 2.0, RoboCasa-GR1, BEHAVIOR-1K, CALVIN, LIBERO-Plus) under a unified server-client evaluation interface. This interface supports both simulation and real-robot deployment, with the goal of bridging the gap between research exploration and practical application.
6.  **Lowering the Barrier to Entry**: By providing simple, fully reproducible single-benchmark training recipes with minimal data engineering, StarVLA intends to lower the effort required for the community to reproduce existing methods, build upon them, and develop new ones. These recipes are designed to achieve competitive or state-of-the-art performance, serving as reliable baselines.

In essence, StarVLA is motivated by the need for a "Lego-like" codebase that fosters an environment where VLA research can be conducted more efficiently, comparably, and cumulatively, moving towards the realization of generalist embodied intelligence.

---

### 4. Methodology and Approach

StarVLA's methodology is centered on the creation of a modular, unified, and open-source codebase designed to streamline Vision-Language-Action (VLA) research. The approach addresses the fragmentation in the field through a standardized architecture, flexible training paradigms, and a unified evaluation and deployment system.

**Unified Framework for VLA Systems:**
The core design principle is a **backbone–action-head decomposition**.
*   **VL Backbone**: This component consumes raw multimodal observations (e.g., visual observations, depth maps, tactile feedback, proprioceptive states, and language instructions). It processes these inputs and exposes hidden-state representations. StarVLA supports both VLM backbones (e.g., Qwen3-VL) and world-model backbones (e.g., Cosmos-Predict2) as drop-in alternatives.
*   **Action Head**: This component reads the representations provided by the backbone and translates them into motor commands. The action head is pluggable, allowing for diverse action decoding strategies.

This framework employs a **unified policy-centric formulation** for VLA models. A VLA system is abstractly modeled as a policy `π` that maps multimodal observation history (`x_≤t`) and a language instruction (`ℓ`) to a future action chunk (`a_t:t+k`) and optional auxiliary outputs (`y_aux`). The training objective `L = L_action + L_aux` allows different VLA paradigms to be interpreted as variations with distinct auxiliary learning signals. For instance, VLM-based VLA might use language-aligned auxiliary objectives, while WM-based VLA incorporates future observation prediction.

**Unified I/O Interface and Compositional Framework:**
StarVLA mandates a unified input/output (I/O) interface for all framework modules, ensuring consistency between training and inference.
*   The `forward()` method for training accepts raw, environment-level observations and returns a loss dictionary.
*   The `predict_action()` method for inference accepts the same raw observation format (without ground-truth actions) and returns predicted action chunks.
This design aims to minimize train/test distribution mismatch and ensure compatibility with real-world robot sensor streams. The compositional framework enables bidirectional modularity: both the backbone and action head can be replaced independently, configured declaratively via YAML.

**Representative VLA Instantiations:**
StarVLA implements four representative VLA paradigms as interchangeable action heads, all sharing the same VL backbone and I/O contract:
1.  **StarVLA-FAST (π_fast)**: Utilizes a FAST tokenizer appended to the VL backbone to autoregressively generate discrete action tokens via next-token prediction.
2.  **StarVLA-OFT**: Attaches a lightweight MLP to regress continuous actions in parallel from the hidden states of predefined action tokens, inspired by OpenVLA-OFT.
3.  **StarVLA-π (π_0)**: Integrates a layer-wise cross-DiT flow-matching action expert for iterative denoising and prediction of continuous actions, conditioned on multi-layer VL hidden states, following π_0.
4.  **StarVLA-GR00T**: Adopts a dual-system design, where the VL backbone serves as a "System 2" (slow reasoning) and a DiT-based flow-matching module acts as "System 1" (fast action generation), consistent with GR00T N1.5.

**Unified System Pipeline for Model Training:**
StarVLA supports several training regimes implemented in PyTorch loops with Accelerate + DeepSpeed for distributed execution:
1.  **Supervised Learning for Behavior Cloning (SFT)**: Standard fine-tuning on robot-only datasets, optimizing the action modeling loss. It supports full-parameter fine-tuning and selective freezing of submodules, with configurable learning rates for different parameter groups.
2.  **Multi-Objective Co-Training for Embodied Reasoning**: Interleaves robot action learning with a VLM loss derived from multimodal web data. This dual-loader scheme aims to preserve general-purpose visual reasoning and language grounding by controlling the trade-off between action learning and VLM capability retention.
3.  **Cross-Embodiment Co-Training with Robot Data Mixtures**: Utilizes a unified LeRobot mixture dataset interface to train on heterogeneous robot datasets. This allows sampling trajectories across datasets with specified weights and tracking embodiment tags.
4.  **Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning**: This is an ongoing integration effort, planned as an extension.

**Evaluation and Deployment:**
StarVLA employs a **unified server-client testing abstraction** for evaluation and deployment:
*   **Unified Server-Client Evaluation Across Benchmarks**: A trained checkpoint is hosted as a WebSocket policy server. Benchmark evaluators interact with this server via a client wrapper, allowing them to remain close to official implementations while standardizing model-side inference. Benchmark-specific adapters translate environment observations to the common StarVLA format and post-process actions.
*   **Deployment on Real Robots**: The same client-server contract extends to real-robot deployment. The robot controller acts as the client, capturing observations, querying the remote policy server, and executing returned actions. This separation allows the model stack and robot control logic to remain distinct.

**Multiple Benchmark Integration:**
StarVLA integrates a diverse suite of manipulation benchmarks, including LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, SimplerEnv, RoboCasa-GR1, RoboTwin 2.0, BEHAVIOR-1K, and CALVIN. For each, it provides a unified integration interface, comprising a checkpoint package, a runnable training entry, and an evaluation workflow, ensuring reproducibility and consistent policy interaction.

**Computation Efficiency:**
The methodology includes profiling measurements for training efficiency on both single-node and multi-node GPU setups (A100/H200). Metrics like wall-clock time per 100K steps, step throughput, and sample throughput are reported to provide guidance on scalability.

---

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The StarVLA project presents several findings based on its unified framework, ranging from single-benchmark performance to cross-benchmark generalization and computational efficiency.

**5.1 Single-Benchmark Training Examples (Section 5):**
StarVLA provides reproducible baselines initialized from public VL pretrained weights and fine-tuned on benchmark-specific datasets without extensive data engineering or online refinement.
*   **LIBERO (Table 2)**: StarVLA variants (FAST, OFT, π, GR00T) achieved competitive average success rates on the four LIBERO suites (Spatial, Object, Goal, Long). For instance, StarVLA-OFT with a Qwen3-VL-4B backbone reached 96.6% average success in 30K optimization steps. This performance is comparable to or surpasses prior methods like OpenVLA-OFT (97.1% in 175K steps) and π_0+FAST (85.5%), demonstrating data efficiency. Performance was also consistent when using a Cosmos-Predict2-2B world-model backbone (average ≥95.2%).
*   **SimplerEnv (Tables 3 and 4)**:
    *   On the WidowX robot (Visual Matching setting), StarVLA-GR00T (Qwen3-VL-4B) achieved an average success rate of 65.3%, while StarVLA-OFT reached 64.6%. These results were competitive with or surpassed established baselines such as GR00T N1.5 (61.9%) and CogACT (51.3%). The Cosmos-Predict2-2B backbone also yielded competitive results (up to 61.6%).
    *   On the Google Robot, StarVLA-OFT (Qwen3-VL-4B) obtained 76.0% average success under Visual Matching, exceeding CogACT (74.8%) and RT-1 (52.4%). Under Variant Aggregation, it achieved 70.2%, remaining comparable to leading baselines. This indicates robustness across simulation evaluation settings.
*   **RoboCasa-GR1 (Tables 5 and 6)**: This benchmark proved more challenging. StarVLA-OFT (Qwen3-VL-4B) achieved the highest average success rate among StarVLA variants at 48.8%, surpassing StarVLA-GR00T (47.8%) and π_0.5 (37.0%). Discrete action variants like StarVLA-FAST performed lower (39.0%).
*   **RoboTwin 2.0 (Table 7)**: All StarVLA variants (Qwen3-VL-4B) demonstrated high average success rates across 50 tasks, with StarVLA-OFT (88.2% clean, 88.3% random) and StarVLA-GR00T (88.0% clean, 88.5% random) performing strongly.

**5.2 Multimodal Co-Training Examples (Section 6):**
A study built on StarVLA's co-training infrastructure investigated the interaction between spatial perception and manipulation performance.
*   **Perception Degradation**: Vanilla VLA, fine-tuned only on action data, showed rapid degradation of perception capabilities (RefCOCO-g IoU@0.5 dropped to near-random levels within 20K steps).
*   **Co-training Benefits**: Vanilla co-training, which interleaved action learning with VLM loss on web data, partially preserved perception and improved manipulation performance (e.g., +4.1% Google Robot VM, +6.4% WidowX).
*   **Spatially Guided Co-training (ST4VLA)**: This advanced variant achieved the best balance, maintaining approximately 70% of original grounding performance (71.2 IoU@0.5 on RefCOCO-g) while simultaneously reaching higher manipulation success rates (84.6%/75.9% on Google Robot VM/VA, 73.2% on WidowX). These results indicate that co-training can counteract catastrophic forgetting of multimodal understanding and lead to more generalizable agents.

**5.3 Cross-Benchmark Training Examples (Section 7):**
StarVLA explored training a single generalist model jointly across multiple benchmark datasets (LIBERO, SimplerEnv, RoboTwin 2.0, RoboCasa-GR1) without benchmark-specific fine-tuning.
*   **Generalist Performance (Table 9)**: The single generalist StarVLA model (using unified padding for action spaces) maintained competitive performance across most benchmarks compared to specialist models trained individually. Notably, it improved the average success rate on RoboCasa-GR1 from the best specialist average of 48.8% to 57.3%. This suggests the feasibility of developing a single policy that can transfer effectively across diverse tasks and embodiments.

**5.4 Computation Efficiency (Section 8):**
Profiling measurements provided insights into StarVLA's training efficiency using StarVLA-GR00T with a Qwen3-VL-4B backbone on RoboCasa-GR1.
*   **Single-Node Efficiency (Table 10, Fig. 5)**: Increasing per-GPU batch size from 2 to 24 on 8 A100 GPUs increased sample throughput from 22.7 to 79.9 samples/s, but also increased step latency from 0.703 to 2.404 seconds/step. A moderate per-GPU batch size (e.g., 8) offered a balance between step latency and GPU utilization (92%).
*   **Multi-Node Scaling Efficiency (Table 11, Fig. 6)**: Scaling from 8 to 256 GPUs with a fixed per-GPU batch size of 8 showed a one-time inter-node communication latency overhead (step time increased from 0.735s at 8 GPUs to ~0.93s beyond 32 GPUs). Despite this, sample throughput scaled near-linearly (from 87.0 to 2200.0 samples/s). Parallel efficiency stabilized at approximately 79–80% beyond 32 GPUs, indicating consistent scalability to hundreds of GPUs.

---

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

StarVLA presents an open-source codebase designed to address significant challenges in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) research, particularly the fragmentation across architectures, codebases, and evaluation protocols. Its potential significance and impact stem from several key aspects:

1.  **Standardization and Reproducibility**: By providing a unified, modular framework with standardized interfaces for VLA architectures, training pipelines, and evaluation protocols, StarVLA aims to enhance the reproducibility of research. This allows for more principled and systematic comparisons between different VLA methods, a crucial step for cumulative scientific progress in embodied AI.
2.  **Accelerated Research and Development**: The "Lego-like" modular design, which allows independent swapping of vision-language backbones and action heads, can significantly accelerate the prototyping and experimentation process. Researchers can readily integrate new models or decoding strategies without overhauling entire systems, fostering innovation and efficient exploration of design choices.
3.  **Bridging VLM-based and World-Model-based Paradigms**: StarVLA explicitly integrates and supports both VLM-based and world-model-based VLA approaches under a common abstraction. This unification facilitates direct comparison and understanding of their respective strengths and weaknesses, potentially leading to hybrid approaches or a more comprehensive theoretical understanding of VLA systems. The proposed "generalized VLA perspective" encourages viewing these as variations within a common framework.
4.  **Enabling Generalist Embodied Agents**: The framework's support for flexible training strategies, including multi-objective co-training and cross-embodiment learning with heterogeneous robot datasets, directly contributes to the development of more general-purpose embodied agents. The demonstrated ability of a single StarVLA model to achieve competitive performance across multiple diverse benchmarks highlights a practical path towards scalable cross-embodiment pretraining.
5.  **Lowering the Barrier to Entry**: By offering simple, fully reproducible training recipes and competitive baselines, StarVLA can make VLA research more accessible to a broader community. This can encourage new researchers to enter the field and build upon existing methods, fostering a more collaborative and open research ecosystem.
6.  **Seamless Research-to-Deployment Pipeline**: The unified server-client evaluation interface, which is compatible with both simulation and real-robot deployment, helps to close the gap between academic research and practical application. This consistency in the interface reduces the engineering overhead typically associated with moving models from development to real-world operation.
7.  **Computational Efficiency Guidance**: The detailed analysis of single-node and multi-node training efficiency provides practical guidance for practitioners aiming to scale VLA model training. The findings on scaling efficiency, particularly the stabilization of parallel efficiency at higher GPU counts, inform resource allocation for large-scale training efforts.

Overall, StarVLA's comprehensive and modular design is positioned to serve as a foundational platform for advancing VLA research, facilitating more robust comparisons, accelerating development cycles, and contributing to the realization of generalist embodied intelligence.
overview:
The development of generalist embodied agents—AI systems capable of perceiving their surroundings, understanding natural language instructions, and executing complex physical tasks—is a central goal of modern robotics. Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as the primary architecture for these agents. However, the field currently faces significant fragmentation. Different research groups use incompatible codebases, varying action-decoding strategies (such as discrete tokenization versus continuous diffusion), and inconsistent evaluation protocols. This lack of standardization makes it difficult to compare methods fairly or to build upon existing work.

StarVLA is a unified, modular, and open-source codebase designed to address these challenges. By adopting a "Lego-like" design, it allows researchers to swap components—such as the visual backbone or the action-decoding head—independently. This modularity enables a systematic exploration of different VLA paradigms within a single, consistent framework.

![StarVLA Unified Framework Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05014v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: The StarVLA framework unifies diverse Vision-Language-Action (VLA) paradigms, including VLM-based, World-Model-based, and policy-centric approaches, into a common architecture with standardized inputs and outputs.*

## A Modular Backbone–Action-Head Architecture

The core innovation of StarVLA is its architectural decomposition into two primary components: the Vision-Language (VL) Backbone and the Action Head.

1.  **Vision-Language Backbone**: This component handles multimodal perception. It processes raw inputs such as camera images, depth maps, tactile data, and language instructions. StarVLA supports diverse backbones, including standard Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like Qwen-VL and generative World Models (WMs) like NVIDIA Cosmos. The backbone transforms these inputs into a high-dimensional hidden state representation.
2.  **Action Head**: This component reads the hidden states from the backbone and translates them into specific motor commands (actions). Because the interface between the backbone and the head is standardized, researchers can plug in different action-decoding strategies without changing the rest of the model.

This separation allows for a "mix-and-match" approach. For example, a researcher could pair a state-of-the-art world model with a diffusion-based action head to study how generative perception affects motor control, or use a standard VLM with an autoregressive head for discrete command generation.

## Unified Mathematical Formulation

To support such a wide variety of models, StarVLA uses a unified policy-centric formulation. In this framework, any VLA system is viewed as a policy $\pi$ that maps the history of multimodal observations $x_{\leq t}$ and a language instruction $\ell$ to a sequence of future actions $a_{t:t+k}$ (often called an action "chunk") and optional auxiliary outputs $y_{aux}$.

The general form of the policy is:

$$\pi(x_{\leq t}, \ell) \to (a_{t:t+k}, y_{aux})$$

During training, the framework optimizes a joint loss function:

$$L = L_{action} + L_{aux}$$

Here, $L_{action}$ represents the error in action prediction, while $L_{aux}$ represents auxiliary objectives. For VLM-based models, $L_{aux}$ might be a next-token prediction loss on text data to maintain language reasoning. For World-Model-based models, $L_{aux}$ could be a video generation loss used to predict future camera frames. This unified loss structure allows StarVLA to train models across different paradigms using the same underlying pipeline.



## Representative VLA Paradigms

StarVLA implements four primary action-decoding paradigms as interchangeable heads. This allows users to compare the most popular methods in the literature directly:

*   **StarVLA-FAST**: Uses discrete tokenization. Actions are converted into "action tokens" (similar to words in a sentence), and the model predicts them autoregressively using the backbone's existing language head.
*   **StarVLA-OFT**: Inspired by OpenVLA, this head uses a lightweight Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to regress continuous action values in parallel from specific hidden states.
*   **StarVLA-π**: Based on the $\pi_0$ architecture, this employs a layer-wise cross-attention Diffusion Transformer (DiT). It iteratively denoises an action signal, conditioned on hidden states from multiple layers of the VL backbone.
*   **StarVLA-GR00T**: Implements a "System 1/System 2" design. The VL backbone acts as the reasoning engine (System 2), while a dedicated flow-matching DiT module handles high-frequency action generation (System 1).

![StarVLA Action Head Variations](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05014v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: StarVLA supports four distinct action-decoding heads—FAST, OFT, GR00T, and $\pi$—all of which can be attached to the same VL foundation model.*

## Standardized Training Strategies

StarVLA provides robust support for three main training regimes, implemented using efficient distributed computing tools like DeepSpeed and Accelerate.

### Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
This is the standard approach for Behavior Cloning. The model is trained on expert robot demonstrations to map observations directly to actions. StarVLA simplifies this by providing "recipes" for common benchmarks, requiring minimal data engineering.

### Multimodal Co-training
A known issue in VLA training is "catastrophic forgetting": as a model learns to move a robot arm, it often loses the general visual reasoning capabilities it gained during its initial pretraining on web data. StarVLA implements a co-training pipeline that interleaves robot data with multimodal web data (image-text pairs). This ensures the agent retains its ability to understand spatial relationships and object categories while learning new physical skills.

### Cross-Embodiment Learning
StarVLA is designed for "generalist" training, where a single model is trained on data from many different robots (e.g., WidowX, Google Robot, Franka). The framework uses a unified data loader that can sample from heterogeneous datasets, tracking "embodiment tags" to help the model distinguish between different robot configurations.

## Evaluation and Deployment: The Server-Client Model

One of the most significant barriers to VLA research is the difficulty of evaluating models across different simulation environments and physical hardware. StarVLA introduces a **unified server-client testing abstraction**.

In this setup, the trained model is hosted on a remote "policy server." The evaluation environment (the "client") captures camera frames and instructions, sends them to the server via WebSockets, and receives the predicted actions in return. This design has several advantages:
*   **Environment Independence**: The model code remains separate from the simulation code, preventing library version conflicts.
*   **Seamless Hardware Transition**: The same client-server protocol used for simulation works for real robots. The robot controller simply acts as the client.
*   **Standardized Metrics**: By using a consistent interface, StarVLA ensures that success rates are calculated identically across different benchmarks.

## Performance and Experimental Findings

The StarVLA team validated the framework across multiple major benchmarks, including LIBERO, SimplerEnv, RoboCasa, and RoboTwin.

### Specialist vs. Generalist Performance
The researchers found that models trained using the StarVLA framework achieve competitive results compared to specialized, method-specific codebases. For instance, a StarVLA-OFT model reached a $96.6\%$ success rate on the LIBERO benchmark. 

More importantly, the framework enabled the training of a **generalist model**—a single policy trained across four different benchmarks simultaneously. This generalist model not only performed well on all tasks but actually improved performance on the complex RoboCasa-GR1 benchmark (from $48.8\%$ for a specialist model to $57.3\%$), suggesting that data from different robots and tasks can provide beneficial transfer learning.

### The Role of Perception in Manipulation
Using the co-training infrastructure, the authors investigated the link between perception and action. They found that "Vanilla VLA" (fine-tuning only on robot actions) caused the model's spatial grounding performance (measured by IoU on RefCOCO-g) to drop to nearly zero. By using StarVLA's co-training and a technique called "Spatially Guided Co-training" (ST4VLA), they were able to maintain $70\%$ of the original perception performance while simultaneously achieving higher manipulation success rates.

![Co-training Performance Analysis](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05014v1/x4.png)
*Figure 3: Analysis of perception versus manipulation performance. Co-training (blue/black lines) prevents the catastrophic forgetting of spatial reasoning that occurs during standard fine-tuning (grey line).*

## Computational Efficiency and Scalability

As VLA models grow in size, training efficiency becomes critical. StarVLA provides detailed profiling of its pipelines. When scaling from a single node (8 GPUs) to 256 GPUs, the framework demonstrated near-linear scaling in sample throughput. While inter-node communication introduces a small overhead in per-step latency (increasing from roughly $0.74s$ to $0.93s$), the overall parallel efficiency stabilized at approximately $80\%$. This makes it a viable platform for large-scale pretraining on massive robot datasets.

![Scaling Efficiency Graphs](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05014v1/x6.png)
*Figure 4: Multi-node scaling performance. The framework maintains high throughput and stable efficiency even when scaled to hundreds of GPUs.*

## Conclusion

StarVLA represents a step toward a more unified and collaborative embodied AI research community. By providing a modular, "Lego-like" architecture and a standardized evaluation pipeline, it lowers the barrier to entry for new researchers and enables existing teams to compare different VLA paradigms more rigorously. Whether the goal is to develop a specialized controller for a single robot or to train a massive generalist agent across hundreds of embodiments, StarVLA provides the necessary infrastructure to scale and iterate efficiently.

keyInsights: A modular "backbone–action-head" architecture successfully unifies disparate VLA paradigms, facilitating flexible prototyping and systematic comparison.
Multimodal co-training strategies are effective in preserving general-purpose visual reasoning and language grounding capabilities while improving manipulation performance.
Cross-benchmark training with a single generalist model can achieve competitive performance and even improve results on challenging benchmarks, indicating potential for scalable pretraining.
originalProblem: Fragmentation in VLA research due to diverse and often incompatible architectural designs, training pipelines, and data processing methods.
Lack of standardized evaluation protocols across different VLA benchmarks, hindering fair comparisons and cumulative research progress.
Difficulty in reusing components and integrating different VLA methods due to tightly coupled codebase assumptions.
results: StarVLA variants achieved strong performance on single benchmarks, with StarVLA-OFT reaching 96.6% average success on LIBERO and StarVLA-GR00T obtaining 65.3% on SimplerEnv (WidowX).
Spatially Guided Co-training (ST4VLA) maintained approximately 70% of original grounding performance while boosting manipulation success rates (e.g., +6.4% on SimplerEnv WidowX).
A single generalist StarVLA model trained across multiple benchmarks improved the average success rate on RoboCasa-GR1 from a specialist's 48.8% to 57.3%.
The framework demonstrated robust computational scalability, achieving up to 2200.0 samples/s throughput on 256 GPUs with parallel efficiency stabilizing at 79–80% for larger setups.
solution: Developed StarVLA, a unified and modular codebase based on a "backbone–action-head" decomposition, supporting both VLM and world-model backbones with pluggable action heads.
Established a unified I/O interface and a policy-centric formulation to abstract and interpret diverse VLA systems consistently.
Integrated a unified server-client testing abstraction for standardized evaluation across multiple benchmarks and real-robot deployment.
summary.summary: StarVLA introduces an open-source, modular codebase designed to unify Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model development by enabling interchangeable backbones and action heads. This framework provides competitive performance on various robot manipulation benchmarks, achieving, for instance, a 96.6% average success rate on LIBERO and demonstrating the ability for a single generalist model to enhance performance across diverse tasks.

title: StarVLA: A Lego-like Codebase for Vision-Language-Action Model Developing
